3: The EIPRO study published recently (EIPRO, 2006) has height- ened the awareness of the environmental impact of food produc- tion and consumption by stating that 20-30% of the overall environmental impact of the total EU economic activities could be allocated to food consumption. Later, another study (Weidema et al., 2008) found that meat and milk consumption was respon- sible for 24% of the total environmental impact within EU. It is well established that among the major food items, beef carries the highest environmental load (Mogensen et al., 2009), and thus there is a need to better understand how different ways of food production impact on the environment in order to recommend improvement measures. Nevertheless, few studies exist to assess the environmental impact of beef produced in a life cycle approach (Casey and Holden, 2006; Johnson et al., 2003; Cederberg and Stadig, 2003; Williams et al., 2006; Ogino et al., 2004, 2006). Among these few studies, Casey and Holden (2006) investigated the global warming potential (GWP) of a range of Irish beef production systems, obtaining varying results of which the lowest GWP score was found for dairy based systems. In addition, the other three, Johnson et al. (2003), Cederberg and Stadig (2003), Williams

et al. (2006), obtained results in line with - if not slightly higher than - those from Casey and Holden (2006). It would be useful to employ the same methodology to investigate a wide range of systems that are considered as typical in Europe (Weidema et al., 2008), and in particular focus on differences between dairy and suckler based beef production. The dairy based beef production is foreseen to decline as a result of reduced number of dairy cows (Animals' Angels, 2009), thus probably leading to an expansion of beef production based on suckler herds. Furthermore in the exist- ing studies the issue of in particular global warming impact related to land use and land use changes has not been addressed specifi- cally. It is a matter of concern since carbon emissions from land use change, especially deforestation, are believed to be a significant contributor to climate change, accounting for 20-25% of total anthropogenic emissions during the 1990s (IPCC, 2000). Due to the high feed and/or land requirement related to beef production, this issue is expected to contribute considerably to the GWP per kg beef as also highlighted by Cederberg et al. (2008) for beef production in a Brazilian context. Likewise, emissions from ruminant enteric fermentation and manure handling may contribute to certain environmental impacts (e.g. global warming, acidification, eutro- phication) (Jacobson et al., 2003). Finally, transportation of material inputs and final products as a component of food chains raises environmental concerns about fossil energy use and associated greenhouse gas emissions (Pirog et al., 2001).

The objectives of this study were thus threefold. The first task was to assess the environmental profile of four different EU beef production systems, three based on dairy bull calves and one on suckler herds. These systems were defined in a report on envi- ronmental improvement potentials of meat and dairy products consumed in the EU-27 (Weidema et al., 2008). The second objective was to identify which component(s) of the "cradle to farm gate" beef production system has/have the largest environmental impacts. This information is useful in finding practical measures to improve environmental performance. The third objective was to preliminarily estimate the magnitude of GWP taking into account land use effects for different beef production systems. The issue of accounting for land-use-related carbon emissions currently still remains a matter of dispute. Nevertheless, there is room for attempts to deal with this research challenge.

6: <header>2.1. Definition of typical EU beef fattening systems </header>

Based on the number of dairy cows in the EU-27 (Weidema et al., 2008), it has been estimated that almost half of EU beef comes from culled dairy cows and the remaining is produced in specialized beef production systems. Taking the latter as a whole, a rough estimate gives that 'suckler beef takes up 70% and 'dairy bull beef takes up the rest (Weidema et al., 2008).

Beef fattening systems differ in a variety of parameters such as the age and weight of the animals at slaughter, housing system (indoor, outdoor or mixed) and the composition of the feed. The major European beef production systems were defined in a work by the European Commission (EC, 2001) in terms of age and weight at slaughter of the animals as well as feeding regime. In general, there are two main categories of production systems, depending on whether they produce beef from suckler calves reared with their mothers or from dairy bull calves reared on milk replacers plus solid feed, e.g. soy meal, barley. For the present study, we have chosen to focus on (1) three types of bull fattening from dairy calves which differ regarding the amount of concentrate used, the amount of land used and the age of the animals at slaughter, and (2) one type of suckler beef production systems with a medium slaughter age of calves. We used the before-mentioned classification which also served as the basis for the work of Weidema et al. (2008) in which the aspects of feed use and land use related to these specialized production systems were modelled.
A brief definition of typical EU beef fattening systems is given below.

The suckler cow-calf system (SCC) is a complex system where the beef produced originates from the suckler cow and its offspring - either bull calves or heifer calves for meat and for replacement. The system, classified as semi-extensive, is a combination of outdoor

grazing in summer and indoor feeding with grass silage and concentrates in winter. Often the suckler system relies on a combi- nation of low productive permanent pasture and highly productive (highly fertilized) grassland. In this analysis, we assumed that the grazing took place on low productive grassland yielding approxi- mately 3000 kg dry matter per ha, whereas conserved forage was produced on highly productive grassland yielding 8000 kg dry matter per ha.

The dairy bull calf system (DBC) is based on the male offspring being separated from the dairy cow soon after birth and reared artificially in the dairy herd until achieving a weight of approxi- mately 55 kg. This assumption was made to account for the recruitment of male calves for entering the beef fattening systems and the environmental consequences of this resource use. The system was further classified into three categories: (A) Bull calves, reared indoor on concentrates; (B) Bull calves, reared indoor on a mixed ration of grass silage and concentrates; and (C) Steers, fattened on a 40:60 mix of outdoor grazing and indoor feeding of grass silage and concentrates. Here grassland for silage was assumed to be highly productive grassland yielding 8000 kg dry matter per ha, i.e. the same as that for suckler system, whereas grassland for grazing was moderately productive grassland yielding 6800 kg dry matter per ha.
Basic assumptions related to the animals in all systems are summarized in Table 1.

10: The system boundary for beef fattening is presented in Fig. 1. It is worth first mentioning that feedstuffs for the animals consist of home-grown grass (silage, grazed grass), home-grown cereals (e.g. spring barley, maize), imported soy meal and minerals. The composition of the concentrated feed (cereals as energy feed and soy meal as protein feed) was derived to include only feed produced from those crops that are affected by an increased demand for feed (Weidema et al., 2008; Dalgaard et al., 2007). As seen in the figure, apart from the main processes at the farm, various off-farm processes e.g. soybean production, mineral feed supplement production, fertilizer production and energy produc- tion for farm operations were also included. Within the boundary, the two levels of the farm - herd level and land level - were defined. The farm land allocated to the herd was defined in a way that all feeds, except protein and mineral supplement, were supplied by the farm itself, and that the land occupied was suffi- cient to utilize the manure produced by the animals efficiently. In relation to protein feed for beef fattening, we assumed that imported soy meal was from Argentina. This assumption was made in the context of an expansion of the area under soybean cultiva- tion in Argentina, from 6 million hectares in 1996 to 14.2 million hectares in 2004 (Pengue, 2006), and a highest increase in

Argentine soybean export projected for the year ahead, 2014 (FAPRI, 2006). A fraction of manure collected from stables or deposited during grazing was considered available for growing crops, thus reducing the use of artificial fertilizers. The assumed substitution of fertilizer-N by manure-N was 20% or 60% depend- ing on whether manure was deposited on grass or collected from indoor housing, respectively (Weidema et al., 2008). In all systems, direct on-farm energy use was considered in two forms: diesel fuel for farm operation and electricity for indoor housing and crop processing. Fig. 1 also shows flows accounted for in the calculations of nutrient balances at the farm gate and also at herd and land level. The farm gate nutrient balance differentiating between farm gate inputs and outputs, in particular, provides a straightforward method of benchmarking to assess the environmental perfor- mance of farming systems.

14: The functional unit used in our study is one kg meat slaughter weight delivered from farms. This selection is satisfactory to capture the performance of different production systems for beef, taking into account the different dressing percentages, i.e. the ratio of slaughter weight to live weight, of the animal produced.

The production of soy meal, a protein concentrate, from soybean results in the co-production of soy oil (see Fig. 1). To estimate the environmental impacts of soy meal, we used system expansion to avoid allocation between soy meal and soil oil. It means that the inputs and outputs associated with soybean production are entirely ascribed to soy meal, and the product system is expanded to include the avoided production of palm oil as the marginal vegetable oil (Dalgaard et al., 2008). In this case, the demand for protein feed in the livestock production was seen as a driver of oilseed production.
In relation to allocation for the 'bought-in' dairy bull calves, we found that a practical partition of the related process was possible.

Under this scheme, the proportion of feed needed to satisfy the mother cow's extra energy requirements due to pregnancy was calculated and assigned to the calves. All the remaining feed was allocated to the milk and the meat from the culled cow which was considered outside the system boundary of the study.

16: Key assumptions in relation to basic inputs and direct emissions per 1000 kg meat produced in the four beef fattening systems are summarized in Table 2. In most cases, agricultural emissions cannot be measured with reasonable accuracy or cost-effectiveness, so they are often obtained by using some modelling techniques. Table 3 describes in detail how various emissions from cattle farms were estimated using state-of-the-art calculation methods. Modelling nitrogen leaching requires in particular detailed data on the N dynamics on the farm. For this purpose, it is necessary to quantify the nutrient balances at herd, land level and at farm gate. A summary of the results of such an analysis performed for the four beef fattening systems is provided in Table 4.

As seen in Fig. 1, the DBC systems get bull calves reared artifi- cially outside as an important input. This input was modelled as a separate process taking into account feed and energy use for rearing calves from 40 kg at birth (day 0) until achieving a weight of 55 kg (day 30) as well as all associated emissions to air and to water (Table 5). Feed composition for calves was modelled to include feed intake to satisfy the mother cow's extra energy need due to pregnancy, amounting to 130 Scandinavian Feed Units (barley equivalents). The analysis in relation to calf rearing also assumed that calves are reared indoors and the manure they produce, considered as a nutrient source, is exported off site to be utilized as fertilizer. The use of manure for fertilization, depending on the environmental regulation in a particular geographic area, may not always be the case but should be considered as a target for resource

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

(2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. (5) For estimating CH4, N20 and NH3 emissions from manure management, an assumption was made that manure in the form of slurry is first stored in slurry pits below animal confinements for 3.5 weeks, and then transferred to separate slurry tanks where it is stored with a natural crust cover. 
(6) NO3-N potentially leached was estimated from the soil balance after deducting airborne emissions and soil N change.</list>

25 and 298, respectively, following IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2007). The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. It is first transported from Rosario Harbour to Rotterdam Harbour, the Netherlands (12,000 km) by ship, and then to livestock feed factories and farms (totally 850 km) by truck. Dairy calves are transported from rearing units to fattening farms (dairy bull calf systems). The distance of transport by trucks is about 30 km, taking into account that the two specialized farms are not necessarily located too far apart. e Factors for estimating on-farm emissions are summarized in Table 3. f Highly and moderately productive grassland was assumed to be a carbon sink of 0.52 Mg C/ha/year, whereas arable land was a carbon source of 0.84 Mg C/ha/year (Vleeshouwers and Verhagen, 2002). Low productive grassland was assumed to be at carbon balance, i.e. no net flux.</list>

conservation. A sensitivity analysis to compare the results with and without this assumption, however, is not necessary considering the low feed intake and emissions from calf rearing (Table 5) relative to those from beef fattening (Table 2).
2.5. Environmental assessment

feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in
