different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. 2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. The Search- inger et al.'s (2008) study provides a package of information rele- vant to the estimate of carbon emissions from land conversion in

different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. 2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

The method, complying with ISO 14040 standard for life cycle assess- ment, was designed for use not only in product development processes but also in environmental assessments of alternative systems including e.g. alternative technology concepts/solutions. Since the EDIP method does not include the impact category "non- renewable energy use', the Impact 2002+ method (Jolliet et al., 2003) was used as a substitute to calculate the results.
2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. different ecosystems in ten world regions, for instance: (1) average biomass and soil carbon stocks in undisturbed ecosystems, (2) ongoing carbon uptake by re-growing forest, and (3) forest area. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.6. The issues of land use and land use change

The issue of land use has recently received increasing attention, both from academic and policy-oriented audiences. In most LCA studies, this land use aspect is presented as how much surface area in combination with a certain time period is required to produce a unit of output (Lindeijer, 2000). This information is useful in a way to assess land use efficiency/productivity by making a comparison between different land-based production systems. There are, however, two somewhat related issues that need to be considered further. First is the so-called 'opportunity cost of land' (Garnett, 2009), that is, the value of land for alternative uses, e.g. forestry. If the land were to revert to forest, it would be most effectively sequestering carbon and therefore the loss of carbon sequestration potential needs to be allocated to forage and cereal crops used for livestock. Second is the potential of land use change resulting from an increased demand for land-based products. It is well recognized that a growing demand for feed to support meat production increases the pressure on land use globally (Stehfest et al., 2009),

potentially driving land use change. Among the major patterns of land use change resulting from an expansion of livestock produc- tion are the conversions of forest to pasture and to cropland as having taken place in Latin America with an intensive rate (Steinfeld et al., 2006). Both land opportunity cost and land use change have GHG implications that require thorough analyses to assist decision makers in land use management.

Despite efforts and improvements made by researchers (Milà i Canals et al., 2007; Köllner and Scholz, 2007; BSI, 2008; Fargione et al., 2008), so far no general agreement has been reached on an acceptable method for evaluating emissions from land use change, especially indirect land sue change where an increased pressure on land resources eventually leads to the conversion of land. Recently an attempt has been made to explicitly account for the aspect of land use in analyzing GHGs embodied in exported soybeans and beef from the Brazilian Amazon (Zaks et al., 2009). In another scholarly publication, Searchinger et al. (2008) estimated GHG emissions in the production of corn-based ethanol in the US using a worldwide agricultural model to account for land use change. Their calculation assumed the loss of all carbon in vegetation and 25% of soil carbon following land conversion from forest to crop- land. Another study (Murty et al., 2002) found that, on average, the effect of land use change from forest to pasture on soil carbon is insignificant. Besides the losses of biomass and soil carbon, the conversion also sacrifices the ongoing carbon sequestration that would take place each year if forests are not cleared. We used the information mentioned to estimate carbon emissions per hectare converted by region, as presented in Table 6. The resulting 'pulse' of carbon emissions was then distributed evenly over a depreciation period of 20 years, as suggested by IPCC (2006) and BSI (2008). The last step was calculating the weighted average of carbon loss considering all forest systems per square meter per year.

An obvious link between imported soy meal used in livestock production and land use change (e.g. conversion of forest into crop- land) enabled a straightforward translation from the area of land use for the feed into CO2 emissions, using the rate of 2.8 kg CO2/n 2 year in Table 6. The impact of land use related to home-grown forage and cereals can be discussed in terms of the opportunity cost of

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

2.5. Environmental assessment
For each system, a worksheet was created in SimaPro 7.1 using the LCA food database (Nielsen et al., 2003) and EcoInvent database (Ecoinvent Centre, 2004) for key system inputs such as imported
feed (soy meal, mineral feed), fertilizers, diesel, electricity and transport.

Five impact categories to be considered by the analysis were Global warming potential (GWP), Acidification potential (AP), Eutrophication potential (EP), Land occupation and Non-renewable energy use. These impact categories are commonly addressed to provide a comprehensive picture of the environmental profile of agricultural products. For the first four categories, the EDIP method (Wenzel et al., 1997) was used to calculate the results with the update of the 100-year Global warming potential of CH4 and N20 to

rearing livestock or growing crops on land if alternative uses of land e.g. forestry are taken into account. To estimate the so-called land opportunity cost for cropland growing cereals and grassland which could have been forest, we assumed the rate of carbon sequestration loss to be 2.8 and 2.2 kg CO2/m2 year (see Table 6), respectively. As per one of our objectives, we conducted a sensitivity analysis to quantify land-use-related GHG emissions for different beef production systems. This was done following an identification and segregation of the results on the impact category 'land occupation (i.e. land use) per kg beef into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). Another crucial task was to derive the ratio of the potential land use change and land opportunity cost to the areas of different land use patterns. How we handled the task is described as follows.

<list>(2) For grass grazed, we assumed a ratio of 0.38:1 and 0.85:1 based on the relative yield of low and moderately productive grass- land to highly productive grassland. Our argument was that permanent grassland provides functions not only for forage production but also for others e.g. preserving nature diversity. It thus appeared reasonable to derive the land opportunity cost of low and moderately productive grassland after an adjust- ment of the area of land use to the yield of highly productive grassland was done. 
Although such assumptions may not reflect precisely reality which is more complex, the results obtained from the sensitivity analysis provide preliminary indications and estimations of the critical load (i.e. maximum load). 
The selection of depreciation period and method of allocating emissions over time is also a matter of debate in literature. Twenty years could be considered typical for some plantations over which the 'pulse' of emissions is distributed evenly or allocated to the years directly after the land use change occurred and gradually decreased (Zaks et al., 2009). In fact, there is no scientific argument against the choice of a longer period (Croezen and Kampman, 2008). For example, Searchinger et al. suggested a period of 30 years considering near-term emission reduction potential of corn- ethanol (Searchinger et al., 2008) and IFEU proposed a period of 100 years which coincides with 4 oil palm plantation cycles (IFEU, 2007). It would be informative to observe the effect of selecting different depreciation periods on the estimates of land-use-related carbon emissions.</list>

47: The LCA results of the four beef production systems are pre- sented in Table 7. A breakdown of contributions to the environ- mental impacts from different components of the chain is performed in Fig. 2. Note that all corresponding results displayed in the figure are in percentages relative to those obtained for dairy bull calf A, which are set as baseline at 100%. A chain-component was defined as a hotspot if it contributes more than 40% to the total of the impact category, as recommended in the work by Dekker et al. (2008). Detailed discussions on the environmental performance range of the four representative systems as well as the environ- mental hotspots in each impact category are given below.
48: Among the four systems, the suckler cow-calf system (SCC) was identified as having the worst environmental performance in all impact categories, with the highest rate of feed use to produce 1 kg of meat and consequently highest rate of manure excretion. The low N and P efficiency of the system also indicated substantial nutrient losses. Beef fattening based on naturally reared suckler calves appeared to be less efficient than that based on artificially reared dairy calves due to the high environmental cost of suckler herd management and the two-stage conversion of feed to milk and then from milk to meat (in the cow and the calf's body, respectively). Remarkably, DBCA had by far the best performance owing to optimum feed use, less manure excretion and conse- quently high nutrient uptake efficiency (which also means less nutrient loss). Coming second and third in the environmental performance ranking of the four systems were DBCB and DBC with modest feed use and manure excretion. The environmental performance of DBCc, in particular, approached those of SCC as the castrated male cows need to be fed (mainly) on grass for a relatively long time in order to reach the desired weight for slaughter.

3.2. Identification of environmental hotspots - brief comparison with other studies
51: The GWP per kg beef produced in the EU ranged from 16.0 to 27.3 kg COe (without land use consideration) depending on systems. The leading contributors to GWP for all systems were direct CH4 and N20 emissions from 'fattening' (48, 55, 78 and 72% for DBCA, DBCB, DBC and SCC respectively), with CH4 as the main contributor accounting for 31-51% of the total GHG emissions. Notably, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation accounted for 73-88% of the total CH4 emissions from fattening. Land-use-related carbon emis- sions accounted for 21,15, -12 and 2% for DBCA, DBCB, DBC and SCC, respectively. The 'negative contribution" for DBC (i.e. deduction of the impact) resulted from the role of highly and moderately productive grasslands as a carbon sink. In contrast, the 'positive contribution' (i.e. addition of the impact) for other systems was
caused by an extensive use of croplands which act as a carbon source.

A brief comparison between GHG emissions from suckler beef production in the EU and those presented in recent literature was done to put this study in perspective. The figure of 27.3 kg CO2e per kg beef in this study is about 8-24% higher than the range of 22-25.3 kg CO2e/kg reported by Johnson et al. (2003), Cederberg and Stadig (2003) and Williams et al. (2006). The figure of 36.4 kg CO2e/kg reported by Ogino et al. (2006) for a Japanese system seemed rather high compared to other studies including ours. However, it should be noted that Ogino et al. (2006) used the retail beef yield percentage of 40% to calculate the environmental impact potential per kg beef produced. If displayed per kg live weight, the figures for GHG emissions per kg suckler beef for the two case studies - EU and Japanese - become rather similar at 15.6 and 14.6 kg COe, respectively. In another study, Casey and Holden (2006) analyzed a number of Irish beef production systems, reporting a variation in GWP from 7.2 to 11.3 kg CO2e per kg beef live weight per year (not including land-use-related carbon emissions/ sequestration), of which the lower score was found for a dairy based system and the upper for a suckler based system. The range in our study, if scaled to the functional unit of kg live weight per year and excluding land-use-related carbon remissions/sequestration, would be 6.6 to 11.4 kg CO2e which is not much different from that in Casey and Holden (2006).

We acknowledge that the reference studies may use the GWP weighting factor of 21 or 23 for CH4 and 310 or 296 for N20, which is different from the factor we used in our study, 25 and 298 for the two non-CO2 GHGs, respectively. The variation due to the use of different GWP factor, however, is not expected to have a major impact on the results of our study relative to references. When we checked the GWP of our SCC system calculated with the current weighting factor against the other two, it turned out only a minor change of 3.7-5.5%.
3.2.2. Acidification potential (AP) In terms of acidification potential, a range of 101 to 210 g SO2e per FU was found for the four beef production systems. The main
Transport of soy meal and calves
Fig. 2. Environmental hotspot analysis for beef fattening systems. Legend: tenvironmental hotspot.
contributor to this was 'direct NH3 emissions from fattening" with a contribution rate of 69.2-85.7%.

For a comparison between the EU and the Japanese suckler beef systems (Ogino et al., 2006), it is necessary to convert the corre- sponding results to g SO2e per kg live weight the reason for which has been discussed earlier. As such, the figure for the EU case would represent 88% of that performed by the Japanese system, 120 versus 136 SO2e/kg beef.

In another comparison, we found that our result on acidification potential of EU suckler beef was approximately half of that obtained by Cederberg and Stadig (2003) for organic beef produced in a typical Swedish system, 210 versus 448 SO2e/kg (originally 14 mol H+/kg). One possible reason for this would be due to the handling of manure in deep straw bedding in the Swedish extended beef system which causes higher ammonia emissions than the handling of manure in the form of slurry considered for EU beef systems.

One possible reason for this would be due to the handling of manure in deep straw bedding in the Swedish extended beef system which causes higher ammonia emissions than the handling of manure in the form of slurry considered for EU beef systems.
53: The GWP per kg beef produced in the EU ranged from 16.0 to 27.3 kg COe (without land use consideration) depending on systems. The leading contributors to GWP for all systems were direct CH4 and N20 emissions from 'fattening' (48, 55, 78 and 72% for DBCA, DBCB, DBC and SCC respectively), with CH4 as the main contributor accounting for 31-51% of the total GHG emissions. Notably, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation accounted for 73-88% of the total CH4 emissions from fattening. Land-use-related carbon emis- sions accounted for 21,15, -12 and 2% for DBCA, DBCB, DBC and SCC, respectively. The 'negative contribution" for DBC (i.e. deduction of the impact) resulted from the role of highly and moderately productive grasslands as a carbon sink. In contrast, the 'positive contribution' (i.e. addition of the impact) for other systems was
caused by an extensive use of croplands which act as a carbon source.

A brief comparison between GHG emissions from suckler beef production in the EU and those presented in recent literature was done to put this study in perspective. The figure of 27.3 kg CO2e per kg beef in this study is about 8-24% higher than the range of 22-25.3 kg CO2e/kg reported by Johnson et al. (2003), Cederberg and Stadig (2003) and Williams et al. (2006). The figure of 36.4 kg CO2e/kg reported by Ogino et al. (2006) for a Japanese system seemed rather high compared to other studies including ours. However, it should be noted that Ogino et al. (2006) used the retail beef yield percentage of 40% to calculate the environmental impact potential per kg beef produced. If displayed per kg live weight, the figures for GHG emissions per kg suckler beef for the two case studies - EU and Japanese - become rather similar at 15.6 and 14.6 kg COe, respectively. In another study, Casey and Holden (2006) analyzed a number of Irish beef production systems, reporting a variation in GWP from 7.2 to 11.3 kg CO2e per kg beef live weight per year (not including land-use-related carbon emissions/ sequestration), of which the lower score was found for a dairy based system and the upper for a suckler based system. The range in our study, if scaled to the functional unit of kg live weight per year and excluding land-use-related carbon remissions/sequestration, would be 6.6 to 11.4 kg CO2e which is not much different from that in Casey and Holden (2006).

We acknowledge that the reference studies may use the GWP weighting factor of 21 or 23 for CH4 and 310 or 296 for N20, which is different from the factor we used in our study, 25 and 298 for the two non-CO2 GHGs, respectively. The variation due to the use of different GWP factor, however, is not expected to have a major impact on the results of our study relative to references. When we checked the GWP of our SCC system calculated with the current weighting factor against the other two, it turned out only a minor change of 3.7-5.5%.

55: The EP per kg beef produced in the EU reached an upper value of 1651 g NO3e for SCC from a lower value of 622 NO3e for DBCA. Among the group of substances responsible for eutrophication, e.g. nitrate, ammonia, phosphate, nitrogen oxides and nitric oxide, nitrate leaching from agricultural soils is by far the most important contributor. As expected, the dominant contributor to EP was 'direct emissions from fattening" (84, 87, and 97% for DBCA, DBCB, DBCc and SCC, respectively), with nitrate as the major contributor over ammonia and phosphate.

The figure of eutrophication potential of the EU suckler beef system as presented in this paper was quite in line with that reported by Cederberg and Stadig (2003), 1651 versus 1842 NO3e/kg (originally 8.2 kg O2e/kg). The figure, however, was about 3.8 times as high as for the Japanese system reported by Ogino et al. (2006), at 941 versus 248 g NO3e per kg live weight. A possible explanation for the difference in the result between this study and the Japanese study may lie in the methodology used for the estimate. In calculating EP, the EDIP method used here considers all emissions to air (e.g. ammonia, nitrogen oxides, nitric oxide) and emissions to water (e.g. nitrate, phos- phate). It is unclear from the Japanese study whether emissions to water of phosphate and especially nitrate were taken into account.

57: The 'non-renewable energy' category is another important indicator of the sustainability of food production systems, given that it comes from finite resources which will eventually be exhausted beyond the level that can be economically extracted. As shown, one kg beef leaving the farm for slaughter consumed 41-59 MJ non-renewable energy. The largest contributor to 'non- renewable energy' for all systems was direct energy use in fattening, accounting for 50-58%. Fertilizer import represented another hotspot for SCC and DBCc with 41.7 and 42.9% contribution, respectively.

As for the suckler cow-calf system (SCC), the figure was comparable to that reported by Elferink (Elferink, 2009) for a conventional system in the Netherlands, but much lower than that reported by Ogino et al. (2006) for the Japanese system. The very high energy use associated with the latter was accounted for by the production and transportation of feed amounting to 64.6 and 31.4% in the cow-calf stage (Ogino et al., 2004) and 63.6 and 34.9% in the fattening stage (Ogino et al., 2006), respectively, of the total. It is worth noting that Japanese cattle are raised and fattened for a rather long period (26-28 months), which often leads to reduced feed uptake efficiency. The energy cost of long distance transport of
feed from the US to Japan adds one more variation making Japanese beef almost two times as energy intensive as EU beef from suckler herds.

59: Land occupation refers to the area of land that is being in use and thus temporarily unavailable for other purposes. The results in Table 7 show that producing 1 kg EU beef required 17- 43 m² year, which was split into different portions of cropland (soy meal and cereals) and grassland (highly, moderately and low productive). As expected from the inventory data in Table 2, SCC was the most land-extensive of the four systems with a land requirement in the upper end of the range at 43 m² year. What made the suckler based system significantly different from the dairy based systems in land use patterns was an intensive use of low productive grassland, 70% out of the total land use. The use complies with EU agricultural policy in conserving low productive grasslands the function of which is not only for grazing but also for preserving nature diversity and landscape. Fig. 2 clearly indicates that the impact of land occupation was mainly, if not primarily, caused by 'direct land use for fattening', where a large input of land was needed to grow either forage or cereal crops for raising cattle. The contribution was highest for SCC (almost 100%) and DBC (94.5%), falling to 72.5 and 66.1% for DBCB and DBCA, respectively. However, if 'land occupation' for total feed use (i.e. home-grown feed plus imported feed) was considered, its contribution for DBCA and DBCB would increase to 91-92%.

The figure of 43 m² year for the EU suckler beef closely agrees with that found by Williams et al. (2006) of 38.5 m² year for beef produced in the UK, but 45% lower than the 78 m² year reported by Cederberg and Stadig (2003) as a typical value for Swedish organic beef production. The relatively high land use intensity of the Swedish beef can be explained by the fact that the feed ration of the animals was based almost solely on roughage fodder produced on-farm, mainly at low yields (Cederberg and Mattson, 2000).

61: <header>3.3. Sensitivity analysis of land-use-related GHG emissions </header>

What needs to be done after estimating the land area required is to assess the consequent potential of GHG emissions. Fig. 3 is a summary of the results, including a check of the effect of changing depreciation period. As shown, over a 20 year depreciation period, GHG emissions with the consideration of land use aspects were about 3.1-3.9 times as high as those without the consideration (84.1, 69.5, 63.6 and 62.4 versus 27.3, 19.9, 17.9 and 16.0 kg CO2e/kg beef for SCC, DBCc, DBCB, and DBCA, respectively). The GHG impli- cations of different land use patterns when taken into account, however, did not change the relative GWP of the four systems. The findings for the choice of a longer depreciation period, 30 years, clearly revealed that the longer the time span over which the land- use-related carbon emissions were divided, the smaller the added GHG emissions.
62: Based on the results of the study, the main conclusions are briefly summarized as follows.

The environmental costs per kg EU beef leaving the farm gate were 16.0-27.3 kg CO2e for GWP, 101-210 SO2e for AP, 622-1651 NO3e for EP, 41.3-59.2 for non-renewable energy use and 16.5-42.9 m² year for land occupation. Of the two fattening categories, fattening based on suckler herds appeared to be less environmentally friendly than if based on intensively
land use change from forest to
Fig. 3. Potential effect of land use on GHG emissions from EU beef fattening systems.

reared dairy calves. The main reason for such an unfavourable performance was the high rate of feed intake to produce the meat, amounting to 19 kg grain equivalent per kg. In dairy bull calf systems, only the proportion of the feed needed to satisfy the mother cow's extra energy requirements due to pregnancy and consequent environmental costs were assigned to her calves, whereas all the remaining feed was allocated to the milk and the meat from the culled cow. The dairy bull calf A had the best environmental performance with the lowest rate of feed use of 5.2 kg grain equivalent per kg beef (live weight), of which protein feed made up the largest proportion compared to other systems (12% versus 7, 0.06 and 0% for dairy bull calf B, suckler cow-calf and dairy bull calf C, respectively).

The breakdown analysis allowed an identification of the key areas in the beef production chain where changes are recom- mended to improve environmental performance. For GWP, feeding strategies have to be identified to minimize enteric methane emissions, since it was a relatively large contributor to this impact category. A roughage-based diet would have a higher dry matter weight and thus produce more CH4 emissions than a concentrate- based diet with the same feeding value. Increasing the proportion of concentrate in a diet would thus help reduce CH4 generation. In addition, nutrient loss in the form of N20 - a greenhouse gas 298 times as potent as CO2 - from manure management and agricul- tural soils also needs proper measures to control. For acidification and eutrophication, initiatives to control nutrient loss via NH3 emissions and NO3 leaching from manure management should be implemented to reduce these environmental impacts. To reduce non-renewable energy use, a proper measure would be anaerobi- cally digesting the manure to produce biogas for energy before applying to fields as fertilizers. This will help enhance farm energy self-sufficiency by avoiding the import of fossil energy as well as reduce direct methane emissions and all emissions associated with the production of fossil energy. The transport of a small amount of soy meal from distant regions and dairy calves from adjacent farms appeared not to be of major environmental concern; it was a minor contributor not only to fossil energy use and GWP, but also to other impact categories. In terms of land use, the results suggested that the dairy bull calf system is better than the suckler herd system for beef production if potential land use change and land opportunity cost are taken into account. The key message from the results of the

sensitivity analysis is that there is a need for discussions and actions in promoting sustainable land use and forest management for climate change mitigation. Nowadays, the number of dairy cows in Europe has decreased as a result of an increased milk yield per cow. If this trend keeps up, more and more beef will have to be derived from suckler herds implying an increase in the environmental loads contributed by beef production. It is time to step up efforts to work out improve- ment measures towards beef production based on suckler herds.
